Principles of Economics explain how people make choices when resources are scarce. In a market, there's many buyers and sellers, and each one try to maximize their own benefit. When the price of a good rise quick, buyers usually buy less, and sellers want to sell more. Me and my friend call this the law of demand and supply.

If the government set a price ceiling, like rent control, sometimes there's not enough apartments for everybody who want one. In that case, people might be fixin' to look for other places, and landlords ain't always willing to rent at that price. So, there's shortages, and people don't get no apartment they want.

The equilibrium price is where the quantity supplied equal the quantity demanded. If the price is above equilibrium, there's a surplus, and sellers can't sell all their goods. If the price is below equilibrium, there's a shortage, and buyers can't get what they want. The market, over time, adjust itself, and eventually they is a new equilibrium.

Sometimes, the government intervene and put taxes or subsidies. If the government put a tax on sellers, the supply curve shift up by the amount of the tax. Sellers try to pass the tax to buyers, but they can't always do it all the way. The burden of the tax depend on the elasticity of demand and supply. If demand is inelastic, buyers end up payin' most of the tax. If supply is inelastic, sellers pay more.

Me and my friend also talk about opportunity cost. When you choose to do one thing, you give up the next best thing you coulda done. For example, if you spend time studyin' economics, you ain't spendin' that time workin' or hangin' out with friends. So, the opportunity cost is what you give up.

In the long run, markets tend to move toward equilibrium, but sometimes shocks or government policies can keep 'em out of balance for a while.
